The main claim is: Crossposted to EA Forum While there have been many previous surveys asking about the chance of existential catastrophe from AI and/or AI timelines, none as far as I'm aware have asked about how the level of AI risk varies based on timeline…
But this seems like an extremely important parameter for understanding the nature of AI risk and prioritizing between interventions.
The main claim is: Even in 2062, we probably will not have any production processors with 50 GHz base clock rate (it may well be considerably slower).

The main claim is: Inside views on AI safety are overrated: they feel far truer than they are, and a disciplined outside view (e.g. deferring to a few respected researchers) often tracks truth better than most people's inside views.
It's more valuable to deeply understand others' views — being able to repeat back a gears-level model they endorse — than to rush to evaluate them.
The main claim is: Instead of talking about alignment research, we should differentiate conceptual alignment research and applied alignment research.

The main claim is: If an AI system has better human-specific cooperative intelligence, it should be easier to (a) align that system with a human principal and (b) have that system cooperate with other humans (besides its principal).
